metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default behavior for private/link-local SSRF guard (e.g. 'warn by default; add `auth_introspection_allow_private_hosts: false` to hard-reject') and add an AC or note it is out-of-scope for this story.
findings.finding: The Security section says the SSRF guard should 'warn/optionally reject' private/link-local hosts, but does not specify which behavior is the default, whether this is config-gated, or how it would be tested. No AC covers private/link-local host behavior; an executor cannot know what to implement.
findings.location: Security section — 'HTTPS-only endpoint (SSRF guard; warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section replace 'warn/optionally reject' with a stated default, e.g.: 'emit a warning log; hard-rejection is future work.' If config-gated, name the key and add it to the `auth_introspection_*` enumeration in What.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the config key that enables the aud-absent override (e.g. `auth_introspection_skip_aud_check: false`) and register it in the `auth_introspection_*` list, or explicitly state that the override is deferred and the verifier always rejects aud-absent tokens in this story.
findings.finding: The Security section describes an operator escape hatch allowing aud-absent tokens when 'the operator has explicitly configured that this AS's tokens are resource-scoped by other means', but no config key is defined for this override, so the executor cannot implement it and the behavior is untestable.
findings.location: Security section — 'fail-closed unless the operator has explicitly configured that this AS's tokens are resource-scoped by other means'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`auth_introspection_skip_aud_check` (default `false`) — when `true`, allows tokens whose introspection response omits `aud`; documented as reducing security guarantees.' Or add a note: 'The aud-absent override is future work; this story always rejects aud-absent tokens.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a config key (e.g. `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`) to the What section's flat-key list and a corresponding AC, or remove the escape-hatch clause from the Security section so the policy is unambiguously 'always reject absent `aud`'.
findings.finding: The Security section describes an operator escape-hatch that allows absent `aud` to be accepted ('unless the operator has explicitly configured that this AS\'s tokens are resource-scoped by other means'), implying a config key or flag, but the What section registers no such config key, and no AC tests this path.
findings.location: Security section (aud paragraph) vs. What section (flat config keys) vs. AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the parenthetical escape-hatch clause ('unless the operator has explicitly configured...') from the Security section if the intent is always-reject, OR add `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` (bool, default false) to the What config-key list, register it in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json, and add an AC asserting that `allow_missing_aud=true` lets an `active:true` response with no `aud` field pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the missing opt-out config key (e.g., `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`, default `false`) to the What section's registered flat keys list, include it in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json registration, and cover it in a unit test asserting accept/reject behavior when the flag is true/false.
findings.finding: The Security section references an operator opt-out for the fail-closed aud-absent behavior ('explicitly configured that this AS's tokens are resource-scoped by other means'), but no corresponding config key (e.g., `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`) is listed among the flat keys in the What section. This leaves the opt-out mechanism unspecified and unregistered.
findings.location: What section (flat config keys) / Security section (aud-absent behavior)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` (bool, default `false`) to the flat keys in the What section, register it alongside the other `auth_introspection_*` keys, and add a unit-test AC variant: 'when `allow_missing_aud=true`, a response omitting `aud` is accepted rather than rejected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that asserts a private/link-local endpoint (e.g. `https://169.254.169.254/introspect`) is rejected at construction or raises on first call, and resolve the 'warn/optionally reject' ambiguity by picking one behavior (recommend: reject at construction, consistent with the HTTPS check).
findings.finding: The Security section requires an SSRF guard ('warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts') but no Acceptance Criterion covers this behavior, and the 'warn/optionally reject' wording leaves the required behavior ambiguous.
findings.location: Security section (SSRF guard) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC 5)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts' with 'reject at construction' and add to AC 5: 'a private-IP or link-local HTTPS endpoint (e.g. https://169.254.169.254/introspect) is rejected at construction with a descriptive error'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the default SSRF behavior for private/link-local introspection endpoints (e.g. 'reject by default; add `auth_introspection_allow_private_hosts` bool key to opt in') and add a corresponding AC or explicitly mark private-host handling as out-of-scope for this story.
findings.finding: The SSRF guard for private/link-local introspection endpoint hosts declares two possible behaviors ('warn/optionally reject') without stating which is the DEFAULT, leaving an implementor free to choose log-and-proceed (warn only), which would permit SSRF to RFC-1918 / link-local targets. AC-5 only tests http:// rejection, not private/link-local host rejection. The default posture on this boundary-crossing security control is undeclared.
findings.location: Security section — 'HTTPS-only endpoint (SSRF guard; warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts)' and AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section replace 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts' with a definitive default, e.g.: 'reject (raise) by default; operators may set `auth_introspection_allow_private_hosts: true` to permit internal AS deployments — when set, emit a WARNING log.' Add `auth_introspection_allow_private_hosts` to the What flat-key list and add an AC-5 oracle that verifies a private-IP https:// endpoint is rejected at construction unless the flag is set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either register a concrete config key (e.g. `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`, bool, default false) in the What flat-key list with a security note and a covering AC, or remove the escape-hatch clause from the Security section and declare that absent `aud` is always rejected with no operator override.
findings.finding: The Security section describes an operator escape-hatch that permits accepting tokens when the AS omits `aud` ('unless the operator has explicitly configured that this AS's tokens are resource-scoped by other means'), but no config key for this override appears in the What section's flat key list, and no AC tests the override path. The security contract for this boundary-crossing bypass is undeclared and unimplementable without an implementor inventing an unregistered key.
findings.location: Security section — 'Audience' paragraph; What section flat config keys; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (simplest, recommended for this story scope): Remove the escape-hatch parenthetical from the Security section and state 'absent `aud` is always rejected; operators whose AS omits aud must configure the AS to include it.' Option B: Add `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` (bool, default false) to the What flat-key list, add it to MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json, add a Security note that enabling it weakens audience binding, and add an AC that asserts accept when the flag is true and aud is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving strategy for the 'cache key is a hash' property, e.g. 'after a successful cache population, assert that the cache dict contains no key equal to the raw token string (assert raw_token not in verifier._cache)' or 'assert the cache key equals sha256(token.encode()).hexdigest()'.
findings.finding: AC-4 asserts that 'the cache key is a hash, not the plaintext token' as an observable oracle, but no concrete proving command or inspection method is specified, making this sub-criterion ambiguous to test in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-4: 'the cache key is a hash, not the plaintext token'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC-4: 'observable oracle for hash key: assert `raw_token not in verifier._cache` (or equivalent) after a positive cache population — the only key present is a digest of the token, not the token itself.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the Scope/What section) whether `mcp.auth_strategies` dispatch is an S2 deliverable or an S4 deliverable; if S4's, add it explicitly to the What section and AC1.
findings.finding: The plan states the IntrospectionTokenVerifier is 'selected by the `introspection` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`', treating `mcp.auth_strategies` as an established config routing mechanism. A full grep of the repo finds zero occurrences of `auth_strategies` anywhere in the codebase or config schema. The plan's Scope section says only 'Depends on S2' without specifying that `auth_strategies` dispatch is an S2 deliverable — if it is, there is no evidence; if it is S4's own deliverable, it is absent from the What/AC sections.
findings.location: What section — 'selected by the `introspection` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Scope confirming S2 delivers the `auth_strategies` dispatch seam, OR add 'implement the `auth_strategies` config key and strategy-router in `_mcp_auth.py`' to the What section and a corresponding AC line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC 2 to assert `sub` and `client_id` are correctly mapped from the RFC 7662 response onto the returned `AccessToken`.
findings.finding: The What section states the verifier maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`, but AC 2 only explicitly verifies `scopes` and `expires_at`; `sub` and `client_id` field mappings have no covering AC.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`') vs. AC 2
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC 2: '…and `sub` / `client_id` are populated from the introspection response fields of the same names'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a max-entries bound (e.g., configurable `auth_introspection_cache_max_size`, default 1000) or specify LRU eviction for the in-process cache, and cover it in the Security/Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan's in-process cache has no maximum-entry limit or eviction policy beyond TTL expiry. A client presenting many distinct tokens (even at small scale) within the TTL window will cause unbounded in-memory growth.
findings.location: Security section: 'Cache: keyed on a hash of the token, TTL = min(configured_ttl, token_exp)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `functools.lru_cache` or a simple `OrderedDict`-based LRU with a configurable max size. State the default (e.g., 1000 entries) in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reliability requirement that introspection transport errors are logged at ERROR level (without token/secret content) and specify at minimum a structured log event (e.g., `introspection_error` with endpoint URL and HTTP status) so operators can monitor and alert on endpoint failures.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no circuit-breaker, no observability instrumentation (logs/metrics/alerts) for introspection failures, and no degraded-mode documentation. When the introspection endpoint is down, every token validation raises and every authenticated request is denied — but operators have no automated signal to detect this condition.
findings.location: Security section (Fail-closed transport); Acceptance Criteria (no observability AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security or Compatibility: 'Log a structured ERROR event on each transport failure (fields: endpoint, http_status, error_type — never token or secret). Provide a counter metric `introspection_failures_total` if the host framework exposes one.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that cache reads/writes are protected by an `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent) and add an AC or test that concurrent calls for the same token result in only one introspection HTTP call.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a mutable in-process cache shared across concurrent async requests but does not address concurrency safety. A check-then-set pattern (look up hash → not found → call endpoint → store result) has a TOCTOU window: two concurrent requests for the same uncached token will both hit the introspection endpoint.
findings.location: Security section: 'Cache: keyed on a hash of the token'
findings.suggested_fix: State in Approach: 'Use a per-token asyncio.Lock (or a single cache-wide lock) to prevent concurrent duplicate introspection calls for the same token hash.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the module-size contract gate as a potential consumer: if `_mcp_auth.py` exceeds 800 lines, add it to `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`, `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`, and `docs/architecture.md` as part of this story's deliverables.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` (enforced by `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` / `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`) is an unenumerated consumer: if `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` exceeds 800 lines — plausible given it must implement the verifier, SSRF guard, client-auth logic, token-hash cache, and TTL math — the structural test will fail until the file is added to the allowlist and ceilings files.
findings.location: Scope section / Approach & rationale

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or operator security guide) that documents the introspection verifier's security model: fail-closed aud-absent default, cache-vs-revocation tradeoff, and HTTPS-only SSRF guard — so the rationale persists beyond the plan ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel security architectural decision — fail-closed behavior on absent `aud` with a configurable opt-out, and the documented cache-delays-revocation tradeoff — but does not plan an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or narrative operator-facing doc section beyond config-key regeneration.
findings.location: Security section / Approach & rationale / AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or AC: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or create `docs/adr/introspection-security-model.md`) documenting the fail-closed aud default, cache tradeoff, and SSRF guard policy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete default timeout value for the introspection HTTP call (e.g., 5s) and add an AC asserting the fake/stub is called with that timeout.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but never states a concrete timeout value or a maximum acceptable latency for the hot-path token validation. This leaves implementors free to choose an arbitrarily large timeout (e.g., 30s), blocking every authenticated request for that duration during an endpoint brownout.
findings.location: Security section: 'bounded timeout'; AC line 7: 'make lint && make typecheck && make test pass'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config key `auth_introspection_timeout_seconds` (default: 5) and assert in tests that the httpx client is constructed with `timeout=5.0`. Document in the Security section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the introspection HTTP call uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (or equivalent async path) and add an AC or test note confirming async-safe invocation.
findings.finding: The plan does not address whether the httpx introspection call uses the async (`httpx.AsyncClient`) or sync (`httpx.Client`) API. If the verifier is invoked in an async MCP server context (the SDK is async-first) and uses a synchronous httpx call, it will block the event loop on every token validation.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: State in Approach: 'Use `httpx.AsyncClient` with `await client.post(...)` to avoid blocking the event loop.' Add a unit-test assertion that the verifier's verify method is `async def`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `auth_introspection_cache_ttl` belongs in the public `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` manifest now (rather than as an undocumented tuning knob), given that caching is off-by-default and explicitly described as undermining the instant-revocation rationale.
findings.finding: The plan adds 4 new flat config keys (auth_introspection_endpoint, auth_introspection_client_id, auth_introspection_client_secret_env, auth_introspection_cache_ttl) to MCP_ENV_VARS, doubling the registry from its current 4 entries, without examining whether a subset could be collapsed or whether any existing key already covers a toggle.
findings.location: What section / Acceptance Criteria AC1: 'The auth_introspection_* flat keys are registered in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (the file that owns the `MCP_ENV_VARS` tuple) as an explicit edit target in the plan's Scope or What section.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` is an unenumerated edit target: the 4 new `auth_introspection_*` flat keys must be added to the `MCP_ENV_VARS` tuple defined there, but the plan names only `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` as targets without identifying the file that owns the tuple.
findings.location: What section / 'Registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What or Scope section, change 'Registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json' to 'Registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`) and `server.json`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` / `docs/env-vars.md` as a consumer to update: confirm the new env reads in `_mcp_auth.py` use a recognized read pattern (or add the helper to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`), and regenerate `docs/env-vars.md` as part of this story.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it AST-scans `src/rebar/*.py` for `os.environ` reads and generates `docs/env-vars.md`; its CI drift gate (`python scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check`) will fail if new env reads in `_mcp_auth.py` use a helper not in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`, or if `docs/env-vars.md` is not regenerated after adding new reads.
findings.location: What section / AC1 'regenerated reference docs'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section that the introspection HTTP POST MUST use `httpx.AsyncClient` (awaited) inside the async `verify_token` method — not `httpx.Client` — to avoid blocking the FastMCP async event loop on every cache miss.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Reuse the already-present httpx' for the introspection POST, but never specifies whether to use `httpx.AsyncClient` (async, non-blocking) or `httpx.Client` (sync, blocking). The FastMCP token-verification interface (`mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier`) is async; every authenticated request triggers the introspection call. If the implementer uses synchronous `httpx.Client` (the simpler default) inside an `async def verify_token`, it will block the event loop on every cache miss — a latency and correctness hazard in a concurrent async server.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security section — 'bounded timeout' / AC-5 ('targets only an HTTPS endpoint')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach & rationale section: 'The introspection POST uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (awaited) because `TokenVerifier.verify_token` is an async method called on every authenticated request; using the synchronous `httpx.Client` would block the event loop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete cache mechanism (e.g., `cachetools.TTLCache` as a new declared dependency, or a stdlib `dict` + monotonic-timestamp approach) so the implementer has an unambiguous prior-art reference and any new dependency is declared in `pyproject.toml`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a TTL cache keyed on a token hash with `TTL = min(configured_ttl, token_exp)`, but never names the mechanism that implements this time-bounded cache. The codebase's only confirmed cache primitive is `functools.lru_cache` (which has no TTL support), and `cachetools` (which provides `TTLCache`) is unresolved/unconfirmed as a dependency. If the implementer assumes `cachetools`, it is an unverified/undeclared dependency; if a manual dict-with-timestamps is intended, the plan leaves the implementation approach entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Security section — 'Cache: keyed on a hash of the token…TTL = min(configured_ttl, token_exp)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Approach & rationale: 'The TTL cache is implemented with `cachetools.TTLCache` (new declared dependency under the `mcp` extra) keyed on `hashlib.sha256(token).hexdigest()`; this is the established pattern for bounded in-process token caches and avoids bespoke eviction logic.' Then add `cachetools` to the `mcp` extra in `pyproject.toml`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
